data.title: Canopy Growth Rallies On Renewed Hope Of Cannabis Rescheduling
data.description:
Canopy Growth (CGC) and other cannabis stocks rallied yesterday on news from the Trump administration that cannabis rescheduling may happen soon. MSOS (MSOS), the US cannabis ETF, gained nearly 20% over the day. Canopy Growth’s stock price increased 17% over the day. The price has gained 25.5% since I covered the company last February on Seeking Alpha. The stock price trades below its book value per share. I continue my rating of a Hold. There is higher risk, and the rating may change at the end of May when Canopy Growth releases its Q4-2026 results.

News on Rescheduling

Last December, Trump signed an EO to accelerate the process of reclassifying cannabis to a Schedule III drug. The actual rescheduling did not occur, and it is unknown whether the process was accelerated because there were no updates. Last Saturday, while signing an EO to boost research into psychedelics for the treatment of mental illness, the president commented that the process of rescheduling cannabis needed to be completed and said that the Department of Justice was “slow-walking” the process.

Yesterday, April 22nd, Axios released a story that claimed that the process would be completed soon. Axios claimed that the source of the news was a White House administration official “familiar with the matter.” This news led to the current rally, but there are still a few unknowns. The news on Wednesday was that it could happen as soon as this week. It is unclear whether the next step would be a new administrative hearing on cannabis led by the DEA or if the attorney general, Todd Blanche, would simply reclassify it without a hearing.

In April, a new pilot program began where Medicare will cover a certain amount of CBD and hemp products for its patients. It would seem beneficial that rescheduling be completed for this program to be fully successful. There has been speculation all month on social media that the process would be completed soon.

Rescheduling cannabis would move the drug from schedule I to schedule III. Schedule I drugs do not hold any medical benefits and are the most restricted drugs. Schedule III is for drugs that have medical benefits. The move would allow a few things to happen with cannabis and cannabis companies. Schedule III would allow cannabis to be formally studied for medicinal applications and benefits. US cannabis operators would be allowed to claim 280E tax exemptions on their business operations. It is possible that, after rescheduling, cannabis companies may have access to more banking services. There is also speculation that rescheduling will ultimately lead to federal cannabis legalization.

Current Synergies

Canopy Growth is positioned to benefit from cannabis rescheduling through its US cannabis holdings. The company owns Canopy USA, which consists of US cannabis assets, including Acreage Holdings, Wana Brands, and Jetty Extracts. Once federal law allows it, Canopy Growth will include Canopy USA’s earnings and operations in its financial performance.

The strongest current synergy for Canopy Growth is its recent acquisition of MTL Cannabis. The company operates grow facilities and a set of brands in Quebec. Canopy Growth plans to use the facilities to boost its international and domestic operations. MTL reports CA$84 million per year in revenue and CA$11 million in operating cash flow. The acquisition will increase Canopy Growth’s revenue.

Q3-2026 Results

Revenue for Q3 was CA$75 million, a 12% increase QoQ and a slight decrease YoY. Canadian medical sales and international sales are driving growth. The company reported a gross profit of CA$21.5 million, a slight increase QoQ and an 11% decrease YoY. Profit margin came in at 29% versus 33% QoQ and 32% YoY. The company reported a net loss of CA$62.6 million versus a net loss of CA$1.6 million QoQ and a net loss of CA$121.9 million YoY.

The company reported CA$371 million in cash and equivalents at the end of Q3. Canopy Growth has CA$225 million in total debt. The company recently opened a CA$150 million credit facility for the purpose of making future acquisitions.

Canopy Growth will report Q4-2026 results on May 29th. The current market consensus for Q4 revenue is CA$79.12 million, which will represent an increase QoQ and YoY.

The cannabis sector rallied on the news that rescheduling may happen soon. Stock prices were up across the board. Canopy Growth increased 21% over the day, while Tilray gained 14%. Organigram and SNDL were up 10% over the day. The rally may subside if no further developments occur over rescheduling.

Canopy Growth lags its peers in terms of revenue and a lack of net income. The company has a strong balance sheet. It holds second place in terms of cash & ST investments. It holds 3rd place in terms of total assets.

All of the Canadian LPs have international operations that are driving growth. Canopy Growth, along with Tilray, Village Farms, and SNDL, is positioned to enter US cannabis markets.

Investment Strategy and Risk

Canopy Growth remains at low risk of poor financial performance. The company has strong synergies and a sound balance sheet. The company’s valuation will increase with the acquisition of MTL Cannabis. International sales will continue to increase performance.

A Hold rating comes at a moderate to high risk. The rally this week will likely subside. The stock price was hovering around $1 per share until recently. A lack of developments for rescheduling will reignite sector weakness. Caution is advised.

Conclusion

The cannabis sector rallied yesterday with news of cannabis rescheduling occurring soon. The source was Axios, and the White House official is unnamed. The idea is that the Trump administration will complete the process of rescheduling soon. It is unclear whether a hearing still needs to occur or if the DOJ will finalize a ruling and reschedule it. The cannabis sector may return to weakness, if there are no new developments. Canopy Growth has strong long-term potential and is currently undervalued. I continue my rating of a Hold.

data.title: Old National Bank: Very Efficient, But Expensive
data.description:
We are well into our update coverage of the Q1 regional bank earnings season. If you are new to our earnings season coverage, we look at the key metrics of regionals to look for signs of any weakness that could suggest the economy is softening. It's been good news overall, as our work has revealed stability across the sector. In this column we check in on Old National Bancorp (ONB). This bank is the sixth-largest commercial bank headquartered in the Midwest, but it also has a growing footprint in the Southeast. The just-reported Q1 results add to the evidence that there really are no signs of an economic slowdown. We note specifically here, folks, the strong credit quality. Let us check in on and discuss the key metrics we follow.

Old National Bancorp Q1 Earnings Headline Results

Net income was a strong $229.6 million, with the GAAP EPS coming in at $0.59. On an adjusted basis, backing out merger-related charges, EPS came in at $0.61. Net interest income was a strong source of the overall revenue of $702.7 million, hitting $580.4 million. While the EPS was a $0.01 beat against estimates, we do note that earnings were weighed down a bit by the provision for credit losses of $34.9 million. These provisions were up slightly from the sequential quarter. Folks, remember that provisions directly impact the bottom line, so we keep a close eye on this. What about loans and deposits?

Old National Bancorp Q1 Loans and Deposits

We always look into the trends in loans and deposits to gauge the health of any bank. Ideally, we like to see increases for both lines, of course. For Old National, these trends are moving in the right direction.

Total loans reached $49.8 billion, which was an 8.0% annualized growth rate organically and up 37% from a year ago. Of course that big jump was due to past acquisitions. The 2% quarter-over-quarter growth was primarily driven by strong, high-quality commercial loan production. Total commercial loan production for the quarter was $3.3 billion, and the bank is sitting on a record commercial pipeline of $5.5 billion.

No signs of a slowdown in demand here. We note that the average yield of 5.8% could change since 90% of loans are floating rate, but we do no see this as an immediate risk right now because the Fed is unlikely to meaningfully reduce rates this year. Markets are pricing in just one cut at this point (you can look at probabilities on the FedWatch tool).

Turning to the deposit base, we saw total deposits were $55.7 billion, up 4.2% annualized. We were pleased to see that deposit costs actually decreased by 8 basis points to 1.72%, although yields on loans are actually down, which weighed on margins. While yields come down when rates are lower, the cost of funds comes down too. It bodes well for margins generally because the cost of deposits continues to come down

While margins are holding up overall (more on this below), they did come down this quarter some. We always expect some noise on this number, but the key to watch for is if the cost of deposits comes down faster than average yields.

Old National Bancorp Q1 Asset Quality and Return Metrics

Asset quality metrics remain pretty solid here too. The company provided a nice slide on this as part of its earnings call:

Net charge-offs were $32.0 million, or 0.26% of average loans, which is very manageable and actually down slightly from the sequential quarter. Nonaccrual loans were also down to 1.03% of total loans, compared to 1.07% at the end of 2025. These are very clean credit metrics, folks. One of the standout figures in this report was the efficiency ratio. The efficiency ratio hit a record 45.7%. As many of you know, we love to see banks with an efficiency ratio under 50%. This 45.7% puts Old National among the top ranks of banks we cover on this key metric. Also informative to why asset quality might hold up is the percentage of where loans are issued geographically and in what segment:

So we see here a good mix. Office has been very questionable for years, and represents just 4% of the portfolio (see the non-owner-occupied commercial loan portfolio pie chart above). Multi-family and rentals make up 17%, which tend to be more stable. Residential real estate and C&I loans, which have been performing well, make up more than half of the loan profile. We view this as stable. Geographically there is diversification. While examining the state economics of each state in the portfolio is beyond the scope of this article, our thesis has been that regional bank earnings have given us a read on different economies within the U.S. We think this suggests we are not seeing major challenges in Minnesota, Illinois, or Indiana, which make up nearly half of the loan portfolio.

Return metrics were also strong. We did mention that margins were weighed down a bit and we note that net interest margin came in at 3.55%. While this did compress by 10 basis points due to the rate environment, it remains at a healthy level compared to many peers. Still, this is one blemish on the quarter, as other peers have expanded margins. That said, the return on average assets expanded to 1.29% from 1.21% in the sequential quarter, while the return on average equity was 11.1% versus 10.4%.

Outlook

The outlook provided suggests slow and steady growth.

Note management assumes no rate cut here even though the FedWatch tool starts to suggest one is possible in the second half of 2026. A single 25 bp cut late in the year probably would not do much to impact results, in our opinion. 15% EPS growth here is respectable for a regional bank. Interestingly, the loan projections were unchanged for 4-6% growth despite 8% annualized growth in Q1. It may be conservative. Finally, the net charge-off ratio expected also adds to the evidence base that we are not seeing signs of weakness in regional banking and, by extension, local economies.

Final Thoughts

Old National Bancorp’s Q1 report is another piece of evidence that the regional banking sector is in good shape. We are seeing continued growth in commercial lending and a well-disciplined approach to expense management. The record adjusted efficiency ratio of 45.7% is particularly impressive in our opinion.

In terms of an investment, we think it is a hold here. The company repurchased 3.9 million shares in Q1 while paying its $0.14 quarterly dividend. But with a book value of $21.17 and a tangible book value of $13.15, the stock is trading at a bit of a premium here. It is hard to find a bank that trades close to tangible book these days, but an 80% premium is excessive in our opinion. Given the current valuation, we view ONB as a quality Hold and would look to be more aggressive buyers on any significant dip, perhaps to $20 or lower.

data.title: JPC: Surviving But Needs Lower Interest Rates To Thrive
data.description:
Overview

When I previously covered the Nuveen Preferred & Income Opportunities Fund (JPC), I issued a hold rating due to the questionable dividend coverage. Despite my caution regarding JPC, the fund has remained quite resilient over the last few quarters. For instance, the share price has only declined by 1%, despite rising pessimism across the debt markets. Furthermore, the fund actually managed to provide a positive return since my last coverage. JPC has released an updated semi-annual report that prompted me to reassess its overall value proposition, outlook, and distribution coverage.

One of the issues I currently have with the fund is that growth potential remains limited in this high-interest-rate environment. Despite this, the fund continues to trade at the higher end of its price-to-NAV range. For instance, the fund now trades at a very tiny discount to NAV of 0.13%. For reference, the fund has traded at an average discount to NAV of 4.62% over the last five-year period. Therefore, this simply isn't the best time to begin accumulating shares. However, the fund's valuation could be a reflection of the resilience it has demonstrated through a period of uncertainty.

Income-focused funds are facing headwinds because high-interest rates introduce default risks. However, JPC's earnings seem to be on par with its typical range. This means that there's a chance JPC will successfully generate sufficient earnings for the 2026 period, despite all of the uncertainty around interest rates. High-interest rates have put a burden on borrowers and have challenged their ability to keep up with their debt maintenance. However, JPC is most heavily allocated to investment-grade borrowers, so the portfolio has demonstrated resilience.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest portfolio overview, JPC has total managed assets of $4.67B that are spread across 257 different holdings. The fund's primary goal is to provide a high current income, which it achieves by investing in a range of preferred and debt-focused securities. Therefore, JPC's success is not correlated to the direction of the market indices, such as the S&P 500. Instead, the success of JPC is directly linked to borrowers' ability to repay their loans over time. This is usually a much easier investment to make since management can implement underwriting efforts against each investment.

JPC also provides some exposure to investments that are based outside of the U.S. However, I wouldn't consider this to be a dedicated global fund since 52.6% of its assets are still allocated to U.S.-focused positions. The remaining 47.4% of assets are allocated to non-U.S. positions across Europe, Asia, and Australia. Expanding on this, the fund also aims to provide some diversity across industries.

However, we can see below that 46.5% of the portfolio is specifically invested across diversified banks. This is followed by an allocation to capital markets and insurance investments, which accounts for 11.4% and 10.5% of assets, respectively. This means that JPC is essentially a financials-linked fund, and this concentration can be a major source of risk if the industry faces any specific headwinds in the future. Likewise, it can also be a strength during periods where financial securities rally higher.

When looking at the positions of JPC, we get further confirmation that this is basically a financials-focused fund. For instance, the largest position is current Barclays plc (BCS) at 1.9% of assets. This is followed by exposure to many other notable banks, including Truist Financial (TFC), JPMorgan (JPM), HSBC Holdings (HSBC), and Wells Fargo (WFC), just to name a few.

Performance Expectations

Since my last coverage, JPC has released an updated semi-annual report that provides some transparency into the portfolio's earnings. Net investment income amounted to $0.25 per share, while net realized gains landed at $0.24 per share. Assuming a similar performance through the rest of the period, JPC is on track to generate the highest level of net investment income since 2022. What I like about JPC is that the net investment income levels tend to be very consistent over time, which is an indication that the portfolio strategy is efficient at generating income through different market cycles.

However, the issue that I do have is that net investment income isn't strong enough to support those distributions on its own. This dynamic isn't necessarily a bad thing, but it does mean that JPC is reliant on net realized gains to fuel NAV growth and support distributions. Net investment income is consistent, while net realized gains can wildly vary based on market conditions. This makes the fund vulnerable to downturns in the market, such as the decline that happened in 2022. JPC experienced a large net realized loss of $1.52 per share in 2022, causing the NAV to decline. JPC still hasn't recovered from this NAV decline:

  • 2022: $21.06 per share
  • 2023: $18.77 per share
  • 2024: $20.13 per share
  • 2025: $20.06 per share
  • 2026 (semi-annual): $20.26 per share

If the NAV fails to grow over time, this will translate to limited capital appreciation for shareholders. For instance, JPC's share price has actually declined by 18.9% over the last decade, even though market indices have rallied to all-time highs over the same time frame. Over the last ten years, there have been very few instances where the share price actually provided meaningful returns for shareholders. When including all distributions that were paid out to investors, the total return jumps up above 82% over the same time frame. Therefore, investors should anticipate that most of their returns will come from JPC's ability to maintain a high rate of payouts going forward.

The fund uses leverage as part of its strategy to amplify returns. Total effective leverage sits at 37.31% of assets, which is equivalent to ~$1.76B. This is an aggressive use of leverage in this high-interest-rate environment, and I believe that it can substantially increase the risk profile of the fund going forward. For instance, this leverage can amplify losses if allocated to a position that declines in value. Furthermore, a high-interest-rate environment means that it is more costly for JPC to use debt. Therefore, the operating spread between its cost of leverage and the yield collected from securities becomes thinner.

Despite the Fed initiating several interest rate cuts through 2025, the federal funds rate still remains near the higher end of its ten-year range. Even during the latest Fed meeting, it was decided that interest rates would be left unchanged. Therefore, my expectations for growth are fairly limited as long as interest rates remain high. Conversely, lower interest rates would serve as a catalyst for growth because they would improve JPC's operating spread for existing positions and create a more favorable borrowing environment, which can translate to an abundance of new growth opportunities in the market for management to capitalize on.

High-interest rates also tend to lead to higher defaults. However, I don't actually think this is an issue for JPC, since net investment income has remained within a consistent range. Additionally, JPC maintains exposure to more investment-grade securities, which have a lower probability of being impacted by defaults since these are higher quality borrowers. For reference, JPC's portfolio by credit rating is as follows:

  • Investment Grade (AAA - BBB): 80.4%
  • Below Investment Grade (BB - D): 17.8%
  • Not Rated: 1.9%

Dividend Risks

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.0625 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 9.4%. Assuming that JPC keeps distributions unchanged for the rest of the year, we can expect the fund to pay out an annual distribution of $0.75 per share. Referring to the same semiannual report, it is clear that the fund is capable of generating sufficient earnings. However, it is ultimately reliant on positive market conditions so that large net realized losses aren't experienced. For instance, JPC reported total earnings of $0.78 per share for its 2025 period, which was enough to support payouts.

Looking over the distribution history for the last decade, JPC has suffered from adjusted payouts through different market cycles. For instance, the distributions were consistently lowered through the pandemic era before eventually getting raised back to their current levels. While this may not be the preferred route for income investors, I believe the decision to adjust payouts is a great move by management. This is because it shows that management is proactive and cares about protecting the NAV. I've analyzed plenty of other funds that refused to cut distributions and caused it to consistently pay out more than it earned, which then eroded the NAV to a point that is hard to recover from.

The caveat is that although I prefer management to be proactive, it still becomes difficult to recommend a position in JPC for income investors. The lowered payouts mean that investors using JPC as a tool for income would have seen their annual dividend income deteriorate. If you are a retired investor, this could negatively impact your livelihood if you're depending on a reliable stream of supplemental income to support lifestyle expenses.

To management's credit, the last few years have been a bit odd for the debt markets. Following the pandemic, we saw interest rates get hiked to their ten-year highs, and now we're experiencing a market that fears defaults, which is a direct result of a prolonged elevated rate environment. So when looking at the most recent UNII report, it comes as no surprise that JPC is struggling to generate sufficient earnings on a YTD basis. From the beginning of 2026 through the end of March, JPC has only maintained a 73.3% distribution coverage rate. If this falls below a coverage rate of ~65%, I do think there is a risk for distributions to be reduced again.

Lastly, investors should be aware that the distributions received from JPC may not offer tax efficiency. The latest available Section 19(a) notice indicates that most of the distributions paid YTD were supported by net investment income, which is classified as ordinary income and has the highest tax burden for investors. Therefore, a position in JPC would be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that JPC remains a hold at this time. The fund has remained resilient, and the NAV has steadily increased from its lows. While net investment income has been demonstrated to consistently fall within a tight range over the last few years, the reliance on net realized gains is a weakness. If we see a downturn in the debt markets, this can cause the fund's NAV to erode as the earnings decline. The fund's latest reporting also indicates that JPC has not generated sufficient earnings to support distributions on a YTD basis. Therefore, I believe there is a risk that distributions will be reduced if net investment income doesn't improve over the next two quarters.

data.title: Thrivent Global Stock Fund Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Executive Summary

  • For the quarter, the Fund outperformed its public benchmark, the MSCI ACWI, by 1.53% and outperformed its Morningstar peer group average by 0.52%.
  • Over the trailing one year, the Fund underperformed the MSCI ACWI by -.07%, but outperformed its Morningstar peer group average by 2.21%.

Performance factors

For the quarter, the Fund outperformed its public benchmark, the MSCI ACWI, by 1.53% and outperformed its Morningstar peer group average by 0.52%. An overweight to equities detracted from performance with weak equity markets overall. Within the Fund, the overweight to large cap growth (large tech/Magnificent 7 names in particular) also detracted with Mag-7 underperforming the broader market by more than 8%, and the underweight to international detracted with international leading domestic by about 5%. The underweight to mid-caps detracted from performance with mid-caps outperforming large caps by about 6%. Offsetting weaker strategic and tactical positioning was strong manager performance from Large Cap Value and International, both of which are large weights in the Fund and strongly outperformed their respective benchmarks. However, somewhat offsetting that positive manager contribution was negative contribution from the Large Cap Growth manager.

Over the trailing one year, the Fund underperformed its public benchmark, the MSCI ACWI, by -.07%, but outperformed its Morningstar peer group average by approximately 2.21%. The overweight to equity added to performance with both domestic and international markets significantly outperforming. Overweight large caps, especially large cap tech and underweight to mid-caps added to performance with Mag-7 outperforming the broader market by more than 12%. On the manager-level, performance was additive, with very strong results from Large Cap Value, followed by International. Underperforming managers included Large Cap Growth.

Trading in January was active, including two trades to reduce domestic and increase international, followed by a trade to reduce large cap growth and increase large cap value, and a trade to sell NASDAQ (short futures) and buy the S&P 100 equal weight via swap. Additionally, we executed several rebalance trades throughout the quarter.

Portfolio outlook

If the Iran conflict proves short-lived, we would remain constructive on markets through year-end. Historically, temporary oil-price spikes are less likely to derail broader growth or consumer spending than sustained energy shocks. The consumer remains reasonably healthy: the latest February retail-sales data showed nominal growth, and inflation-adjusted spending appears to be growing modestly. Importantly, the savings rate remains relatively low by historical standards, suggesting consumers still have capacity to support spending through a lower savings buffer, although this also implies reduced resilience to a negative shock.

The labor market remains in a low-hire, low-fire equilibrium, which leaves less cushion if conditions weaken suddenly, but as long as employment remains stable, household spending should stay relatively resilient. Corporate fundamentals also remain supportive, with earnings at historically elevated levels and showing signs of improvement across large-, mid-, and small-cap companies, helping to underpin equity market resilience and broaden participation. The recent market pullback has also improved the backdrop by resetting valuations to more reasonable levels and modestly reducing market concentration following the outsized leadership of mega-cap equities.

Looking ahead, we expect the U.S. economy to remain supported by tax-related fiscal tailwinds from the July 2025 tax bill, elevated tax refunds, accommodative financial conditions, contained interest rates, solid liquidity, and capex that is holding up. Absent a more material recession, a sustained oil shock, or a market decline sufficient to impair the wealth effect, these conditions should continue to support risk assets and enable a renewed broadening of market participation beyond large-cap technology into value, mid/small caps, and international equities.

data.title: Cartesian Therapeutics: A Phase 3 mRNA CAR-T Bet
data.description:
If you have been following my articles at all, then you know I am a massive fanboy of CAR-T and particularly the emerging field of CAR-T for autoimmune diseases. My first article I wrote about the company that is spearheading this movement, Kyverna Therapeutics (KYTX), which I rated a “Strong Buy.” At the time of writing this piece, that pick is up around 15%. I have also written about an allogeneic oncology CAR-T play in Caribou Biosciences (CRBU), which I slapped a “Buy” rating on, mainly because I think the valuation is quite nice. And now, I am back to writing about another CAR-T autoimmune developer, closer to Kyverna’s wheelhouse.

mRNA CAR-T

Just to go ahead and give everyone a quick refresher, CAR-T treatment aims to reengineer a patient’s T-cells so that they will eliminate harmful B-cells that cause all sorts of problems. To this day, CAR-T has only been approved in the oncology sector to target specific B-cell-driven cancers like types of lymphoma and leukemia. Cool, a nice existing $4.6-8.9 billion market and projected to be $15 billion-44 billion by 2030. Yet, those numbers are just for the oncology sector. Research has shown that a not insignificant number of autoimmune diseases may stem from malignant B-cells that create unnecessary antibodies. These antibodies incorrectly attack healthy people. Thus, why not use CAR-T for some of the 50 million Americans who have autoimmune diseases? This, my readers, is where I think the alpha might lie.

Now, there are a wide variety of companies researching CAR-T for autoimmune, including Kyverna, Cabaletta Bio (CABA), Novartis (NVS), Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY), and AbbVie (ABBV). Only Kyverna is somewhat close to having an approved therapy (BLA imminent), but other players are working hard to catch up and have differentiators. One of these players is Cartesian Therapeutics, who aims to collect patient T-cells autologously but administer an entirely different reengineered treatment by using mRNA.

Ok, what does mRNA do? Think Covid vaccines. In a remarkable feat of turnaround speed, the only reason Covid vaccines were available so soon is that mRNA was safe, and for speculative biotech companies dealing with complex treatments, safety is so crucial. Traditional DNA CAR-T products, like what Kyverna’s lead asset is, require chemotherapy called lymphodepletion, while Cartesian’s mRNA approach eliminates this completely. For cancer patients with relapses, chemo is not as scary because it is more common in oncology. The risk/reward skews to the reward when one looks at the risk of not treating the cancer. However, for many autoimmune patients, the risk is not so huge. Even patients with refractory disease are typically not in mortal danger, so these people are less likely to want to end up in a specialized inpatient transplant center. Disclaimer here, I do understand that although autoimmune diseases may not pose so much mortal danger, they can still make life extremely difficult. For instance, I am still bullish on Kyverna because an inpatient transplant center is a small price to pay for SPS patients who regained their ability to walk!

Anyway, this core differentiator from the safety profile of Cartesian’s mRNA approach cannot be understated. Because mRNA is transient and does not permanently integrate into the genome, this means

  • No chemo
  • No risk of cancerous transformation from genomic integration
  • Fully outpatient administration
  • Controllable dosing

For autoimmune patients, I believe that this safety profile and convenience are a massive draw. But, of course, does it work?

Data: Descartes-08

Cartesian’s lead product is Descartes-08, designed to target antibody production in diseases like myasthenia gravis (MG), systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), and myositis. Given their RMAT and orphan drug designation, Cartesian has made the most progress in its clinical trials on highly symptomatic patients with generalized MG.

The Phase 2b MG trial enrolled 36 patients, randomized 1:1 to Descartes-08/placebo for six infusions without chemo. The primary population consisted of 26 patients at academic centers. Of course, hugely important to the entire idea of CAR-T treatment, 12 patients were evaluable for 12-month follow-up data. For both oncology and autoimmune, CAR-T hopes to offer something that is more of a one-and-done instead of a lifelong course of biologics. The current lead biologic for MG is efgartigimod (Argenx), which is a good drug; however, the patient must take the drug forever or watch their disease come back. And argenx has a crippling price range between $450,000 and $650,000 per year. Then, the important thing for Descartes-08 to get right was having efficacy on par with argenx while maintaining those results months and years later.

Metric

Efgartigimod (ADAPT—best-in-class biologic)

Descartes-08 (Phase 2b—biologic-naive subgroup)

MG-ADL reduction at primary endpoint

~3–4 points (at 28 days / cycle 1)

7.1 points at Month 12

Minimum symptom expression (MSE, score 0 or 1)

Not a published primary metric

57% at Month 6, maintained to Month 12

Durability after treatment ends

Returns to baseline ~4 weeks post-dose

Sustained at 12 months after a single course

Redosing required

~4.7 cycles/year indefinitely

Single 6-week course (re-dosing not yet defined)

Preconditioning chemotherapy

None

None

Administration setting

IV infusion, clinic

IV infusion, outpatient clinic

Annual cost (approximate)

$450K–$650K/year ongoing

Single course pricing TBD (likely $100K–$300K)

You are welcome to interpret the data yourself, but a 7.1-point MG-ADL reduction in the biologic-naïve group not only passes argenx but blows it out of the water. A score of 7 means patients went from struggling to swallow and lift their arms above their heads to eating normally and breathing normally. And, even crazier, that improvement was still there 12 months in, with no additional treatment. That, my friends, is why I love to write about biotech. Article after article, I get to detail a real miracle that offers to change lives in a way that did not exist before. Again, the possibility of a curative treatment overshadows anything that biologics currently offer.

But, I must admit that the hype ought to be viewed critically, and there are a few caveats we should be aware of. First, this is not a head-to-head trial. This Phase 2b trial used a different population, different entry criteria, different endpoints, etc., than argenx trials. Though the biologic naïve group is most comparable to argenx, it had a sample size of n=7. Also, only 12 months out is somewhat short for something that is supposed to be curative. I would have liked to see 12 years out. And to be frank, that small sample size is why this stock has not mooned yet. Those results are great, but will they be replicated in the FDA SPA Phase 3 AURORA trial that is enrolling 100 patients? Ah, well, this seems to be the trick for so many biotech companies; everything hinges on a binary event.

To expand a bit on the ongoing Phase 3 AURORA trial, Cartesian got written agreement from the FDA under Special Protocol Assessment (SPA) that the design of the trial is good enough to support a future Biologics License Application (BLA). That designation helps reduce regulatory risk. Some of the details are as follows:

  • Randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled
  • 100 participants with generalized MG
  • The primary endpoint is to demonstrate an MG-ADL improvement >= 3 points at Month 4
  • First patient enrolled in May 2025, enrollment on track

So yeah, full steam ahead for Cartesian. They have $126.9 million in cash to supposedly last them into mid-2027, and the Phase 3 readout is expected late this year or early 2027. To verify the mid-2027 estimate, we will need to get into the nitty-gritty of that cash runway. For 2025, Cartesian spent $58 million on research and $31 million on general administrative purposes, which equates to $89.5 million total. Assuming quarterly burn stays consistent with something like Q3 2025 ($21.5 million), this implies roughly six quarters of runway at current burn. Ah, but what a bold assumption to think that quarterly burn will stay the same as Phase 3 enrollment ramps up. The research budget already jumped from $45.1 million in 2024 to $58 million in 2025, a 29% increase driven by AURORA ramp-up. If burn accelerates an additional 15-20%, the practical runway shortens most certainly to Q1 2027. All in all, the cash should cover AURORA, but dilution is essentially guaranteed after the completion of the trial.

Very briefly, I will also mention that Cartesian has two other indications: SLE and myositis. Though the phase 2 open-label SLE trial produced solid results, Cartesian paused further enrollment to focus on MG (phase 3) and myositis (phase 2 planned). I point out these indications merely to show the robustness of Cartesian’s pipeline, which I think reduces the risk of all the value being tied up in MG.

Leadership

Speaking of value, part of my research into these biotech companies always has me looking at the leadership team. Who are the brains behind the biotech? In some cases, like Caribou Biosciences, leadership will include people who actually pioneered these techniques. For Caribou, that was Jennifer Doudna and Rachel Haurwitz, scientists that helped develop CRISPR.

In Cartesian’s case, Timothy Springer is a board director, and he did decades of immunology research. He essentially helped build the understanding of how CAR-T cells recognize and engage targets. Having Springer’s reputation in mind, I raised my eyebrow when I saw he had made 27 straight purchases of Cartesian stock, with the last being a $1.52 million purchase between April 9 and 11, 2025. He has personally put in more than $14 million towards Cartesian, which is probably down around 60% from his initial investment, and yet he has never sold a single share. The read-on Springer’s commitment is undeniably bullish for Cartesian; the man who backed Moderna ten years before COVID owns 37% of the company and continues to add.

Notably, Yescarta, the first-ever approved CAR-T therapy (for oncology), came out of NCI, where Springer has deep connections. Speaking of Yescarta, Adrian Bot, the chief scientific officer (CSO) at Kite that helped propel Yescarta to being FDA approved, now sits on Cartesian’s board. After Kite was acquired by Gilead (GILD), Bot founded Capstan Therapeutics as the CSO, which AbbVie bought out for $2.1 billion on hopes that in-vivo CAR-T is the future. Bot knows both the science and the business of CAR-T. Another board director is Kemal Malik. He spent about 25 years at Bayer (BAYRY) and oversaw 20 consecutive positive Phase 3 trials, establishing Bayer’s business unit for cell therapies and mRNA tech. Needless to say, the people who are involved with Cartesian are super smart, and they add value to the company just by their own merits. It is difficult to imagine a group of people like this failing.

Smart Money

Institutional investors and insiders own a whopping 82% of Cartesian’s shares (yes, that includes Springer). I would think that this suggests that the company’s story is well known in professional circles.

  • Wells Fargo increased holdings by ~50% in Q4 2025
  • Alpha Quest LLC increased its position by ~64% in Q3 2025
  • Miller Financial Services increased its position by ~17% in Q4 2025
  • R squared Ltd. established a position in Q4 2025

Not that this truly matters, but Wall Street is bullish on Cartesian. BTIG Research has the highest rating with a $44 price target, maintained November 2025. Cantor Fitzgerald, Wedbush, and Needham all reaffirmed their buy/overperform ratings in March 2026 with ranging targets from $16 to $42. Still, please take these ratings with a grain of salt. Sometimes, these analysts price targets for pre-approved biotechs carry wide uncertainty, so they should be treated as scenario analysis. Yes, 400% upside would be nice, but that really depends on a perfect Phase 3 readout and execution after the fact.

Valuation

A little snapshot of the company financials is as follows:

  • Cash and cash equivalents: $126.9 million
  • Shares outstanding: ~26 million
  • Market capitalization at ~$7/share: $182 million
  • They are losing money… biotech preapproval (net loss of $130.3 million for 2025)

Basically, some quick math will show you that $182 million – $127 million = ~$55 million, and that the market ascribes $55 million to Cartesian’s entire AURORA Phase 3, its pipeline candidates, and intellectual property. I mean, given that the MG market can be valued at $6.1 billion and grow to $10.3 billion by 2034, that valuation is pennies should Phase 3 data be good.

So, with those numbers in hand and everything that I mentioned before, here are my bear, bull, and base scenarios:

Scenario

Price Target

Key Assumptions

The Math

How It Unfolds

BEAR

$1.00–$2.50

~80% downside

AURORA outcome: Misses primary endpoint OR FDA rejects trial design

Dilution: Forced raise of $100M+ at ~$1.50–2.00/share; ~50–65M new shares issued

Total diluted shares: ~75–80M

Valuation basis: The market assigns value to cash only; the pipeline is written off entirely

Residual cash (post-burn): ~$80M

Diluted shares: ~75–80M

Pipeline value assigned: $0

Implied market cap: ~$80–200M

Implied: $80–200M ÷ 80M shares ≈ $1.00–$2.50/share

AURORA data disappoints; ≥3-pt MG-ADL improvement at Month 4 not achieved, or FDA finds fault with design. Phase 2b's n=7 biologic-naive result fails to replicate at scale in a 100-patient trial. Emergency capital raise at distressed prices severely dilute existing shareholders. Remaining pipeline (myositis, Descartes-15) provides only a modest floor above full wipeout.

BASE

$8.00–$15.00

~15–115% upside

AURORA outcome: Meets primary endpoint, and data is broadly consistent with Phase 2b

Dilution: Orderly raise of $75–100M at $5–8/share post-data; ~12–20M new shares

Total diluted shares: ~38–46M

Valuation basis: Market re-rates as de-risked late-stage asset; 1–2x peak revenue multiple applied

MG market size (2024): $6.1B

Realistic peak market share: 5–8%

Implied peak revenue: ~$300–500M

Pre-revenue multiple applied: 1–2x sales

Implied market cap: ~$400–600M

Implied: $400–600M ÷ 42M shares ≈ $9.50–$14.30/share

AURORA meets its primary endpoint with data comparable to Phase 2b. The company raises capital from a position of strength at $5–8/share post-readout. BLA filed. Myositis Phase 2 (TRITON) is producing early encouraging signals. Market values Cartesian as a credible first-mover in mRNA CAR-T autoimmune. Still not yet commercial, but with a clear, de-risked regulatory path ahead.

BULL

$25.00–$40.00

~260–470% upside

AURORA outcome: Statistically robust; biologic-naive 7.1-pt MG-ADL result confirmed at scale

Dilution: Same as base—raise of $75–100M at $5–8/share; ~12–20M new shares

Total diluted shares: ~38–46M

Valuation basis: M&A or partnership. Precedent: AbbVie paid $2.1B for preclinical Capstan. A validated Phase 3 mRNA CAR-T platform in autoimmune commands a meaningful premium.

M&A scenario (low): $1.0B

M&A scenario (high): $2.0B

Diluted shares: ~38–42M

$1.0B ÷ 40M shares: ~$25/share

$2.0B ÷ 40M shares: ~$50/share

Implied standalone or M&A: $25–$50/share range

AURORA demonstrates statistically robust data matching or exceeding Phase 2b at scale. Myositis TRITON shows early pivotal-quality signals, expanding the addressable market well beyond MG. Partnership or M&A discussions initiated, precedent being AbbVie paid $2.1B for Capstan (preclinical in vivo CAR-T). A Phase 3-validated mRNA platform in autoimmune, with RMAT designation, is a strategically attractive asset for any major pharma lacking a cell therapy footprint.

Risk it for the Biscuit

Hopefully you have noticed the price target for my bear case, which shows that the stock could lose up to ~80% of its value. This is common for biotech companies, but Cartesian has a list of specific threats. A few of these risks include a poor Phase 3 readout, dilution at an unfavorable price, competition from other companies like Kyverna, pausing their SLE pipeline, poor trial design or misleading patient populations, low trading volume, and in vivo CAR-T leapfrogging autologous/allogeneic plays. Specifically, dilution is not an if but a when. Should Cartesian dilute into positive results from Phase 3 AURORA, then dilution becomes less of a risk for shareholders. Despite these threats, I think Cartesian has a lot of positives. They have the money to get through Phase 3. They have smart leaders and huge institutional ownership. But, probably most importantly to my thesis, they are in the business of CAR-T for autoimmune, which could very well change the world.

Takeaway

Despite these threats, I think Cartesian has a lot of positives. They have the money to get through Phase 3. They have smart leaders and huge institutional ownership.

The key question is, does the current enterprise value, around $55 million after subtracting cash, fairly represent a company with an FDA SPA-agreed Phase 3 in a market worth billions? I say no, which is generally why I am rating it a buy. Given that the base case I laid out above has a range between $8 and $15/share, I imagine that accumulating anywhere below this range is reasonable to build a position. Furthermore, near net cash value would put the stock at approximately $5/share, which provides some downside protection, making the case for asymmetric upside. Investors ought to understand that biotech can be brutal, and position sizing should reflect the reality that an 80% loss could occur should AURORA fail. Some may even feel inclined to wait for confirmation after the Phase 3 readout or news that points toward the readout being positive before starting a position. Ultimately, I am sized in a position that I can stand to lose, but so far CAR-T for autoimmune has proven to be much more than a lottery ticket, and I suspect that CAR-T will continue to reward investors and patients alike. The possibility of creating a curative treatment to displace biologics for life demands a Buy rating.

data.title: Pzena Global Value Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Global equities began the quarter on solid footing but subsequently reversed course amid concerns surrounding the software sector, driven in part by new AI developments from Anthropic (ANTHRO). These concerns, coupled with an escalating conflict in the Middle East, contributed to broader unease about the global economy and the private credit market. In terms of contribution to return, the United States was our portfolio's weakest-performing region, declining mid-single digits. While we outpaced the broad market benchmark, we underperformed the value series.

During the quarter, the largest contributions came from the materials and energy sectors, as oil prices broke above $100 per barrel. U.S.-based chemicals giant Dow, Inc. (DOW) was the largest individual contributor for the period. With approximately 65% of its ethane crackers utilizing low-cost natural gas feedstock, the company is well positioned to benefit from higher oil prices. Memory chip producer Samsung Electronics (SSNLF) was the second-largest contributor, as consensus estimates for 2026 and 2027 continued to rise, driven by a more than 100% increase in spot memory prices. Finnish telecom equipment manufacturer Nokia (NOK) was higher on investor optimism surrounding its AI-RAN (Artificial Intelligence 3 /Radio Access Network) prospects, while its Network Infrastructure unit continued to exhibit solid growth.

The portfolio's largest detractors were in the financials sector due to concerns about economic weakness, as well as in the health care sector. While IT services company Cognizant (CTSH) reported strong bookings and market share gains, investor focus shifted to the sharing of AI-driven productivity gains with clients, which weighed on the stock. Health insurer Humana (HUM) also detracted during the quarter, as the company disclosed an unexpected 25% increase in Medicare Advantage plan membership, eliciting concerns of adverse selection; however, growth in Humana's 4-star plan membership should help offset some of the added medical costs. Capital One (COF) also detracted, as management guided to higher expenses related to the rollout of the recently acquired Discover Financial Services network in Europe. The stock was also impacted by broader concerns around the financials sector stemming from private credit risks and a potential economic slowdown driven by higher energy prices.

We did not initiate any positions in the quarter, but we added to Cognizant, Humana, Daikin Industries (DKILY), and Skyworks (SWKS), all on weakness. To fund these investments, we continued to trim positions in Samsung, ArcelorMittal (MT), and Dow, all on strength.

Recent performance has been challenged by a combination of macroeconomic concerns, uncertainty around the impact of technological change across industries, and idiosyncratic developments weighing on short-term fundamentals of large, high-conviction holdings. Despite these headwinds to recent performance, we believe the current environment provides exceptional long-term opportunity, given the portfolio's starting point of valuation and the breadth, magnitude, and quality of the opportunity set.

data.title: Lazard Emerging Markets Equity Advantage Portfolio Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Executive Summary

  • During the quarter, the Emerging Markets Equity Advantage Portfolio outperformed its benchmark, the MSCI Emerging Markets Index (net of fees), as stock selection and sector positioning both contributed to the excess return.
  • Stock selection was strongest in the information technology and energy sectors. Consumer staples and real estate holdings lagged.
  • Within sector positioning, the overweight exposure to information technology and underweight exposure in communication services names proved beneficial, although the underweight exposure to financials stocks detracted value.
  • From a country perspective, stock selection was strongest in India and Taiwan and weakest in Korea and Thailand.
  • Alpha was favorable in the quarter, driven by strong results in January and February. Value measures were favorable in every month and the largest source of excess return. Sentiment also contributed, despite a drawdown in March coinciding with the market reversal. Overall, quality barely lagged for the period, helped by a strong February result. Growth measures were weak, underperforming in each month.

Market Review

Emerging equity markets, as measured by the MSCI Emerging Markets Index, ended the quarter broadly flat, declining 0.2%, following a sharp 13.1% selloff in March, the worst monthly decline since the onset of the global pandemic in March 2020. Strength in the US dollar and higher global interest rates contributed to the downturn. Markets had started the quarter strongly, but sentiment deteriorated after the outbreak of the conflict with Iran and the resulting disruption to shipping through the Strait of Hormuz. Brent crude saw its largest monthly increase on record, raising concerns that elevated energy prices could slow global economic activity. The impact was particularly acute for emerging Asian economies, many of which are significant importers of oil from the Middle East.

Within Asia, South Korea remained highly volatile, including a record single-day decline of 12.2% on 4 March. Despite the sharp selloff, the market finished the quarter up more than 19%, the strongest performance among major markets. Corporate governance reforms and heavy retail investor participation continued to support the market. In China, PMI data at quarter-end exceeded expectations, but equities declined nearly 9% as rising energy costs and continued weakness in domestic consumption weighed on sentiment. Structural challenges stemming from the prior real estate overbuilding cycle continued to dampen investor confidence. Indian equities fell more than 18% during the quarter, as the country's technology sector failed to participate in the global AI-driven rally. Weakness in the rupee and rising inflation also reduced the likelihood that the Reserve Bank of India will cut interest rates, which have increased by more than 100 basis points over the past year. Nonetheless, India's GDP growth remains above 6% and is among the strongest in major emerging economies. Taiwan advanced more than 9% for the quarter despite a sharp pullback in March, though its technology sector remained sensitive to higher energy costs.

Latin American markets were among the primary beneficiaries of the global disruption, rising 14.6% during the quarter as the oil- and commodity-heavy exchanges rallied. AI-driven demand for critical minerals such as copper and lithium provided additional support alongside higher oil prices. Brazil was a notable beneficiary, although the country continues to grapple with inflation near 5% and interest rates approaching 15%, alongside rising debt levels. Mexico also delivered positive returns, supported by continued nearshoring trends a strengthening manufacturing activity. The country reported fourth-quarter GDP growth of 3.5%.

Markets in the Middle East produced mixed results during the quarter. Countries located closer to the Iran conflict experienced losses as supply chains and distribution routes were directly affected. Saudi Arabia, which benefits from alternative export routes and infrastructure, rose 9.2% during the quarter. In South Africa, economic conditions remained challenging, as concerns over political corruption and the potential financial bankruptcy of Johannesburg offset gains from higher gold prices.

European emerging markets posted positive returns for the quarter despite the March selloff. Turkey led the region as inflation declined to a four-year low and corporate earnings remained strong. The central bank reiterated its commitment to combating inflation despite political pressure from the government. In Greece, profit-taking following two years of strong gains contributed to a 6.5% decline. Improvements in the country's banking sector and sovereign credit profile supported its upgrade to developed market status.

From a sector perspective, information technology and energy stocks led market performance during the quarter, while communication services and consumer discretionary lagged. Despite the March selloff, risk-oriented factors performed well overall, with higher volatility stocks outperforming. Value factors outperformed, while growth lagged. Sentiment weakened during March but remained positive for the full quarter. Quality factors provided relative resilience during the market decline but underperformed on a full-quarter basis.

What Helped

  • Delta Electronics (DELTY) (2.0% weighting in the Portfolio) continued its strong momentum in the first quarter, rising another 40% despite a 6% dip in March. The company, which supplies cooling systems for NVIDIA (NVDA)'s power shelves, saw annual revenue increase by nearly 38%. It has positioned itself as the leading supplier of liquid-to-liquid cooling distribution units. Delta Electronics enjoys a nearly 35% operating margin, nearly 15 points above its competitor. The company is looking to expand its operations in Thailand and Mexico as well as the United States.
  • Electrical Industries (0.9% weighting in the Portfolio) reported a 57% increase in profit for 2025 and declared a special dividend, which helped the stock to surge. The stock has surged more than 2x in the past year as energy demand, driven in part by AI usage, continues to grow.
  • Samsung Electronics (SSNLF), which has a 6.7% weighting in the Portfolio, declined following the outbreak of conflict in the Middle East, as escalating tensions raised concerns about the stability of key energy supplies, including crude oil and liquefied natural gas. Higher energy costs could weigh on AI data center economics and slow the pace of AI infrastructure buildouts, while semiconductor fabs in Taiwan and South Korea may face additional margin pressure from rising LNG prices.

What Hurt

  • Canara Bank (CNBKY) (0.5% weighting in the Portfolio) dipped after it reported a 25% increase in profit but missed its net interest income and net interest margin targets. It also reported a large increase in other operating expenses, including high technology capex and an accelerated depreciation charge. Projected lower interest rates in India are also expected to reduce the bank's lending margins.
  • Korea Electric Power (KEP) (0.7% weighting in the Portfolio) was adversely impacted by the rising energy prices. As retail electricity tariffs are heavily regulated, the company has limited ability to pass the costs through to its ratepayers, causing the stock to sell off more than 30% in March.
  • Hyundai Motor (HYMTF) (0.1% weighting in the Portfolio) soared nearly 40% in the quarter despite a 38% selloff in March, reflective of the highly volatile Korean market. The limited exposure to the industrial conglomerate hurt the portfolio. The stock ranks poorly in terms of both valuation and growth potential.

Outlook

The escalation of conflict in the Middle East and the resulting closure of the Strait of Hormuz represent the type of geopolitical shock that had the potential to disrupt equity markets in 2026. The immediate surge in oil prices was a predictable response to the effective removal roughly 20% of the global oil supply from the market. Should prices continue to rise as competition intensifies for a constrained resource base, the implications for the global economy could be significant. European and Asian economies appear particularly exposed, increasing the international incentive to restore maritime access through the Strait as quickly as possible.

While Iran currently holds a tactical advantage through its ability to disrupt shipping, we believe sustained external pressure—from regional Gulf states, global trading partners, and Iran's own economic needs—combined with the continued degradation of military and logistical infrastructure by the United States and Israel, could ultimately push the parties toward negotiations that reopen the Strait. The domestic political backdrop in the United States, including midterm elections and limited public support for an extended conflict, also suggests a willingness to compromise. That said, the probability of this outcome remains highly uncertain, and a range of alternative scenarios could result in further escalation.

If the Strait reopens and peace is achieved, the resolution will likely act as a near-term tailwind for equity markets. However, normalization may take time as supply chains adjust and corporations reassess energy security, potentially accelerating investment in diversification of energy inputs and supply resilience.

Beyond the immediate geopolitical developments, productivity gains associated with artificial intelligence continue to support the medium-term earnings outlook, with the potential to stimulate broader consumer demand outside the United States. At the same time, elevated government deficits—and their implications for interest rates—remain a key macro variable. Stronger global growth could partially offset these pressures by supporting fiscal sustainability. Against a backdrop of geopolitical uncertainty and evolving macro risks, we believe diversification remains the most effective portfolio defense. In this environment, disciplined bottom-up security selection is likely to be the most reliable path to identifying attractive opportunities across global markets.

data.description:
Fund performance

The Fund gained (gross of fees) and outperformed the Russell 2000 Index.

At the stock level, shares of Viavi Solutions (VIAV) benefited from growth in the data-centre ecosystem and contributions from recent acquisitions. Everus Construction Group (ECG)'s shares rose after the company issued above-consensus guidance, supported by growing data-centre demand and solid execution. Magnolia Oil & Gas (MGY)' shares benefited from a rise in the crude oil price, driven by geopolitical supply concerns.

Conversely, Alphatec Holdings (ATEC)' shares sold off after reporting fourth-quarter earnings below elevated investor expectations, alongside heightened scrutiny across high-growth medical device names. Shares of Q2 Holdings (QTWO) fell amid continued investor concerns regarding potential artificial intelligence-driven disruption. ADMA Biologics (ADMA)' shares declined after management failed to directly address key concerns on revenue quality, leading to sustained pressure on the stock.

Market review

For the first quarter, US small caps gained marginally and outperformed broader US equities, which fell over the period. US equities were relatively resilient earlier in the quarter. However, this strength gave way to a sharp downturn toward quarter end due to a volatile March. Uncertainty over tariffs and softer economic data contributed to weaker sentiment, while the Iran war was the main driver of the March sell-off.

Military strikes involving Israel and the US against Iran contributed to a sharp escalation in regional tensions. Iran subsequently launched retaliatory strikes across the region, leading to airspace closures and the blockage of most oil shipments via the Strait of Hormuz. Crude oil prices rose sharply, raising concerns over oil supply and renewed inflationary pressures.

US President Donald Trump opted not to impose tariffs on European countries in relation to the Greenland dispute. Subsequent proposals to raise this rate to 15% contributed to renewed uncertainty around the global trade outlook.

During the quarter, the US Federal Reserve (Fed) kept the federal funds target range at 3.50%–3.75%. Fed Chair Jerome Powell reiterated the central bank's commitment to its 2% inflation goal, as measured by the core Personal Consumption Expenditures Price Index. The Fed's latest 'dot plot' showed that policymakers expected to cut rates only once in 2026, while investors expected rates to remain on hold. President Trump also nominated former Fed governor Kevin Warsh to become Fed Chair from May 2026, pending Senate approval.

Activity

We initiated several positions. Garrett Motion (GTX) is a leader in the global turbocharger market. We expect the company to benefit from diversification into industrial applications such as heating, ventilation and air conditioning, and data-centre cooling.

Mirion Technologies (MIR) is a global leader in radiation detection and measurement. We believe Mirion is well positioned to benefit from the accelerating 'nuclear renaissance' as global capacity expands and the installed base continues to age.

Patrick Industries (PATK) is a leading component solutions supplier for original equipment manufacturers across recreational vehicle, marine and manufactured housing industries. We think the company is well positioned for volume normalisation as demand improves.

Quaker Chemical Corporation (KWR) is a global pure-play in industrial process fluids with a highly sticky, service-driven business model. Strong new-business wins and early benefits from cost-reduction initiatives have supported operating momentum. We expect this strength to continue as the company streamlines its network and expands in higher-growth regions.

Steven Madden (SHOO), a leading designer and marketer of footwear and accessories. We believe the company is well-positioned to benefit from consumer discretionary spending, which continues to support demand across its core brands.

Conversely, we sold our positions in ADMA Biologics, Corcept Therapeutics (CORT), SM Energy (SM), Vertex (VERX) and Workiva (WK).

Outlook & strategy

March was defined by sweeping changes in global trade dynamics and a shift in market focus from speculative policy outcomes to the tangible economic consequences of geopolitical conflict. Escalating tensions in the Middle East have pushed energy prices sharply higher, driving renewed concerns over supply-driven inflation at a time when the labour market is beginning to soften. These pressures threaten to temper the growth expected to emerge from the US Congress' 'One Big Beautiful Bill Act' and have revived stagflation concerns. Together, these developments have created a more complex and fragile macroeconomic backdrop for both policymakers and investors.

These pressures were reflected in recent market behaviour, where factor-driven movements have, at times, outweighed company-specific fundamentals. While this environment can pose short-term challenges for active managers, it also creates opportunities for disciplined, fundamental investors to identify and capitalise on valuation dislocations. We believe these conditions are particularly supportive for active management within domestic small- and mid-cap equities, where inefficiencies tend to be more pronounced. Accordingly, we remain optimistic about the long-term outlook for this asset class, which we believe is well positioned to benefit from secular trends such as increased infrastructure investment, accelerating technological innovation and the reshoring of supply chains. The strategy's emphasis on higher-quality businesses provides additional resilience, helping to mitigate macroeconomic headwinds while supporting sustainable growth. In this context, we believe patience, selectivity and a long-term focus remain critical as market volatility creates opportunities for differentiated outcomes over the cycle.

data.title: WTI Crude Oil At Risk Of Mean Reversion Decline Below $102.25 After 5% Spike
data.description:
By Kelvin Wong

The US-Iran ceasefire agreement extension by US President Trump to an indefinite period is in a “fragile state” as the US waits for Iran’s new proposal to kickstart another round of peace talks.

The US and Iran have continued to be locked in a battle for control of the Strait of Hormuz, a crucial chokepoint for global energy flows, with both sides blocking the waterway in a “game of poker” to gain leverage during the extended ceasefire.

On Wednesday, April 22, 2026, Iranian navy forces fired on commercial ships in the Strait of Hormuz while the US intercepted two Iranian-registered oil tankers.

WTI crude oil futures rallied 5% on a false alarm attack in Tehran

In today's (Thursday, April 23, 2026) early Asian session at around 8.00 am Singapore time, there was an unconfirmed social media post on X that highlighted sounds of an explosion heard across Iran, leading to fears that the US-Iran extended ceasefire has ended (see Fig. 2).

West Texas crude oil futures traded on NYMEX spiked up almost 5% within 15 minutes to print an intraday high of $97.22/barrel (see Fig. 1), leading to minor risk-off activities in today’s Asian session (S&P 500 E-mini futures -0.5%, Japan’s Nikkei 225 +0.4%, Hong Kong’s Hang Seng Index – 1.1%, AUD/USD -0.2%) at this time of writing.

Thereafter, a social post on X stated that the earlier explosions turned out to be a drill and a test on the Iranian air defence system, and there were no attacks in Tehran.

The price action of the West Texas crude oil futures has trimmed its intraday gains to 1.3% to trade at an intraday level of $94.27/barrel.

Technical analysis suggests that the current spike up in West Texas oil is more likely a "noise", and the minor sideways range configuration from April 14, 2026 to April 17, 2026 remains intact.

Here are the key factors to support this narrative.

WTI crude implied volatility remains subdued, and backwardation has reduced

The WTI crude oil ETF volatility index (OVX) measures the market’s expected 30-day implied volatility in crude oil prices, derived from near-term USO ETF options.

So far, it has started to print a series of “lower highs” since the April 7, 2026 level of 98.79, towards a value of 76.77 as of April 22, 2026, putting a near-term ceiling on higher oil prices (see Fig. 3).

The recent steep rallies in oil prices seen during the onset of the US-Iran war have been accompanied by a negative WTI crude oil calendar spread, where the 12-month futures price is less than the spot price.

A negative spread (backwardation) signals perceived near-term supply shortages, where immediate demand is pushing spot prices above futures.

The backwardation (WTI 12-month futures minus spot) is now at -19.90 as of April 22, 2026, less than its recent peak backwardation level of -41.57 printed earlier on April 2, 2026 (see Fig. 2).

Let’s now focus on the potential short-term trajectory (1-3 days) of WTI crude oil.

WTI Crude Oil - Retested 20-day moving average resistance, at risk of mean reversion decline

The recent push-up seen on the West Texas oil CFD (a proxy of the WTI crude oil futures) from its range support zone of $86.50/81.94 has reached its range resistance zone of $96.44/98.35 (also the 20-day moving average).

At risk of a mean reversion decline back towards the range bottom, watch the $102.25 short-term pivotal resistance at $102.25, and a break below $90.50 reinforces the minor mean reversion decline scenario to retest the range support zone of $86.50/81.94 (also the 50-day moving average) (see Fig. 4).

However, a clearance with an hourly close above $102.25 invalidates the bullish scenario for a squeeze up towards the next intermediate resistance at $107.12/110.87.

Key elements to support the near-term bearish bias on WTI crude oil

  • The hourly RSI momentum indicator has flashed out a prior bearish divergence condition at its overbought zone (above the 70 level) and staged an exit below it.
  • The 22% rally from its April 17, 2026 low of $81.94 has reached the 50% Fibonacci retracement of the prior down move from the April 7, 2026 high to the April 17, 2026 low that confluences with the 20-day moving average resistance.
